title: Somatic Therapy for Panic Attacks: What to Do When Your Body Takes Over
excerpt: Learn how somatic therapy helps you regain control during panic attacks by tuning into your body’s signals and calming your nervous system.
body:
Understanding Panic Attacks: When Your Body Takes Over

Panic attacks aren’t just in your head — they’re a full-body experience. You might feel your heart race, your chest tighten, or a wave of dizziness. These symptoms can be terrifying, especially when they strike without warning. But what’s really happening is your nervous system is hijacking your body, triggering a survival response that feels out of control. This is where somatic therapy offers a lifeline.

Why Traditional Approaches Often Fall Short

Many people seek therapy for panic attacks and get cognitive-based treatments, which can help reframe thoughts but often miss the physical roots of panic. As Bessel van der Kolk highlights in The Body Keeps the Score, trauma and dysregulation lodge in the body, not just the mind. If your body’s alarm system is stuck in a high-alert state, talking alone won’t reset it.

Somatic therapy focuses on the body’s sensations, nervous system cues, and physical responses. It recognizes that panic attacks are a somatic event — your body’s way of signaling distress and dysregulation.

The Nervous System and Panic: A Polyvagal Perspective

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory is foundational for understanding how the nervous system responds during panic. The autonomic nervous system has three main states:

  • Ventral Vagal: Safety and social engagement
  • Sympathetic: Fight-or-flight activation
  • Dorsal Vagal: Shutdown and freeze

Panic attacks are often a hyperactivation of the sympathetic nervous system, flooding the body with adrenaline and cortisol. You feel trapped in this fight-or-flight mode, which makes calming down difficult.

Somatic therapy helps you identify which state you’re in and learn tools to shift back toward the ventral vagal state—where your body feels safe and regulated.

Somatic Therapy Techniques for Panic Attacks

Somatic therapy offers practical tools to work directly with your body’s experience during and between panic attacks. Here are some key techniques:

1. Grounding Through Sensation and Movement

Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, emphasizes sensing into your body to re-establish safety. During a panic attack, try these grounding steps:

  • Notice your feet on the floor and press them down gently.
  • Feel the contact between your body and the chair or surface you’re on.
  • Slowly move your fingers or toes to reconnect with your physical presence.

These simple acts help interrupt the panic loop and bring you back from dissociation or overwhelm.

2. Breath Regulation

Breathing is a powerful lever for nervous system regulation. Peter Levine, developer of Somatic Experiencing, teaches that slow, deep, diaphragmatic breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system.

Try this:

  • Inhale slowly through your nose for a count of 4.
  • Pause briefly.
  • Exhale through your mouth for a count of 6.
  • Repeat for several minutes.

This extended exhale signals safety and helps downshift sympathetic arousal.

3. Tracking Sensations and Emotions

Deb Dana, a leading Polyvagal therapist, encourages tracking bodily sensations and emotions nonjudgmentally. During moments of panic or anxiety, ask yourself:

  • Where do I feel tension or tightness?
  • What emotions come up with these sensations?
  • Can I allow these feelings to be present without trying to push them away?

This mindful, compassionate awareness helps you build a new relationship with your body—one based on curiosity rather than fear.

4. Resourcing and Safe Place Visualization

Francine Shapiro, creator of EMDR, and others have shown that creating a mental safe place or pulling up a positive bodily resource can counterbalance panic sensations.

Try picturing a place where you felt safe and calm, or recall a comforting physical sensation (warmth, heaviness, softness). Focus on that experience for 30 seconds to a minute, allowing your nervous system to shift.

Why Somatic Therapy Works When You’re Stuck

If you’ve tried talk therapy and still feel trapped in panic cycles, somatic therapy can help you unlock what’s stuck below awareness. Trauma or chronic stress can embed in the body’s nervous system, creating patterns of hypervigilance and dysregulation. According to Gabor Maté, unresolved emotional pain often manifests physically, and healing requires addressing both mind and body.

Somatic therapy doesn’t just manage symptoms — it helps you retrain your nervous system to respond differently. Over time, this rewiring creates a new baseline of calm and resilience.

Integrating Somatic Practices into Your Everyday Life

Consistency is key. Regular somatic practices build nervous system flexibility, making panic attacks less likely and less intense. Here are some ways to integrate somatic healing daily:

  • Body scans: Spend 5 minutes noticing physical sensations without judgment.
  • Movement: Gentle yoga, walking, or stretching to reconnect with your body.
  • Breathwork: Incorporate slow breathing exercises into morning or evening routines.
  • Journaling: Track sensations, emotions, and triggers to increase awareness.

Dan Siegel’s concept of "mindsight"—the ability to perceive your internal world—grows stronger with these practices, boosting regulation and emotional balance.

When to Seek Professional Somatic Therapy

Self-directed somatic work is powerful, but sometimes panic attacks require guidance. A trained somatic therapist can tailor interventions to your unique nervous system patterns and trauma history.

Look for therapists trained in Somatic Experiencing, Sensorimotor Psychotherapy (Pat Ogden), or Polyvagal-informed approaches (Deb Dana). They can help you safely access and release stored tension and trauma, moving you beyond panic toward lasting regulation.

Pull Quote

“Panic attacks are a somatic event — your body’s way of signaling distress and dysregulation.” — Bessel van der Kolk

Final Thoughts

Panic attacks are terrifying because they feel like your body has taken control away from your conscious mind. Somatic therapy helps you reclaim that control by tuning into the body’s language and calming the nervous system. It’s not about forcing yourself to "just relax" or "think positive," but about learning how to listen, respond, and regulate from the inside out.

By integrating somatic practices, you can reduce the frequency and intensity of panic attacks, build resilience, and reconnect with a sense of safety in your own body. This approach honors the wisdom of your nervous system and gives you tools to heal beyond the limits of talk therapy alone.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help if I’ve already tried traditional therapy for panic attacks?
faqs.a: Yes. Somatic therapy addresses the physical, nervous system-based roots of panic, which traditional talk therapy often misses, helping you break free from stuck patterns.

faqs.q: How quickly can somatic therapy reduce panic attacks?
faqs.a: Results vary, but many people notice initial relief within weeks of consistent practice. Lasting change typically requires ongoing work to retrain the nervous system.

faqs.q: Are there risks to trying somatic techniques on my own during a panic attack?
faqs.a: Generally, somatic techniques are safe, but if you feel overwhelmed or retraumatized, it’s best to work with a trained therapist to guide your process.

faqs.q: What if I can’t identify physical sensations during panic attacks?
faqs.a: This is common. Start with small grounding exercises like feeling your feet on the floor or gentle movement, then gradually build awareness with practice.
